Automated CI follow-up (Azure build [1121747](https://dev.azure.com/mssonic/build/results?buildId=1121747) — `Test impacted-area-kvmtest-t2 by Elastictest`):

The Elastictest run failed on `generic_config_updater/test_apply_patch_perf.py|||2` with `RUN_TEST_CASE_FAILED`. This PR only adds the `t1-isolated-d28u4` topology (`ansible/vars/topo_t1-isolated-d28u4.yml`, two j2 templates, one `generate_topo.py` mapping entry, one `tests_mark_conditions.yaml` entry) — none of which exercise the GCU `apply_patch_perf` path. Treating this as an unrelated/flaky failure and not modifying code. Please re-run when convenient (`/azp run Azure.sonic-mgmt`).

Unrelated CI failure: t2 Elastictest reported a failure in `generic_config_updater/test_apply_patch_perf.py` plus a cancelled `crm/test_crm.py` (revoked after 18.71 min). This PR only adds the `t1-isolated-d28u4` topology files and touches no generic_config_updater / crm code, so these look like a flake/infra timeout. Requesting a re-run.

@dayouliu1 @BYGX-wcr  could you pls help review?

LGTM. Verified the truth table against the OR'd condition block:

- Arista-7260CX3-C64 (TH2): broadcom & not -B -> SKIP (correct)
- Arista-7060X6-64PE-B-* (TH5): broadcom & has -B -> RUN (correct)
- Arista-7060X6-64PE non-B: broadcom & not -B -> SKIP (correct)

Condition #1 already handles non-broadcom/mellanox/vpp and the release filter, so no regression on older releases. CI is fully green and already has 2 approvals — safe to merge.

Minor (non-blocking): the `-B` substring match relies on Arista not shipping a future non-TH5 SKU containing `-B` in the name. A regex like `Arista-7060X6-64PE-B(-|$)` would be safer, but current style matches the rest of this YAML.

## Responses to review comments (post-merge follow-up)

Thanks @wsycqyz @StormLiangMS @ZhaohuiS for the thorough reviews!

### Shiyan (wsycqyz)

**#1 platform_asic exact match**: This is the established convention across the codebase (e.g. `fill_leakout_plus_one`, `sai_qos_tests.py`). All Cisco-8000 SKUs return `cisco-8000` as `platform_asic`. No change needed.

**#2 Backport**: This is a master/internal branch only change. Backport is not needed at this time.

**#3 Counter field comments**: Good point — will add SAI counter field names as comments in the `check()` branches. Addressed in follow-up PR.

### Storm (StormLiangMS)

**#1 Env var removal**: `INGRESS_DROP_USE_PG_COUNTER` was a debug-only variable created during investigation. It was never used in any production pipeline. Removal is safe.

**#2 port_buffer_drop scaffolding**: Acknowledged. This is intentional scaffolding — `port_buffer_drop` is a valid noise-immune alternative for platforms without per-PG counters (e.g. Broadcom). Will enable after validation, tracked by #24738.

**#3 setUp validation**: Good point — will add `VALID_COUNTER_MODES` validation in `setUp()` for fail-fast. Addressed in follow-up PR.

**#4 Dead code del**: Agreed — executor now reads `counter_mode` from `__init__` parameter, not from `ptftest` attribute. The `del self.mock_ptftest.ingress_drop_counter_mode` is a no-op. Will remove in follow-up PR.

**#5 Naming**: Fair point on '3-level fallback' vs 'platform-keyed selection'. Will consider refining in a future cleanup.

**#6 CI pipeline comment**: Good catch — will restore the CI pipeline navigation comment. Addressed in follow-up PR.

All code fixes tracked in follow-up PR (pr17).

@yxieca  Thanks for the review, but this looks like a false positive from the AI agent — the PR doesn't add any skip/xfail/skip-style logic. There is no `pytest.skip`, `pytest.xfail`, `pytest.mark.skip`, `skip_when`, or any other test-bypassing construct anywhere in the diff. The full diff is one file (`tests/common/fixtures/grpc_fixtures.py`) and what it actually does is:

1. **Supervisor readiness check** — replaces `time.sleep(3)` + a single-shot `supervisorctl status gnmi-native` probe with `wait_until(30, 1, 0, _supervisor_running)`. Same failure mode: still raises an `Exception` if `gnmi-native` never reports `RUNNING`. No skip.

2. **End-to-end TLS readiness** — wraps the two existing `grpcurl` probes (`list` and `gnoi.system.System.Time`) in `wait_until(60, 2, 0, ...)` / `wait_until(30, 2, 0, ...)` with `-connect-timeout 5 -max-time 10` per attempt. Same assertions (`"gnoi.system.System"` must appear in `list` output; `"time"` must appear in the `System.Time` response). Same failure mode: still raises an `Exception` with the last-attempt rc/stdout/stderr if the probes never succeed.

The motivation is a real race on slow armhf platforms (Nokia-7215 / marvell-prestera, see PR description for the Kusto hits): supervisor's default `startsecs=1` reports `RUNNING` after 1 second of wrapper-script uptime, but `/usr/sbin/telemetry` on that platform needs longer than 1 s to finish `sonic-cfggen -d -t .../gnmi_vars.j2` → `jq` → `exec` and bind the TLS listener. PTF's `grpcurl` then fires inside that window and gets `connect: connection refused`. The fix bounds the existing readiness probe with polling instead of a fixed sleep — strictly *more* checking, not less.

Happy to chat further if there's a specific line that triggered the heuristic.

- `gnmi/test_gnoi_file.py` → **PASSED**, 1/1 test, 452s. The new `wait_until`-based readiness probes (supervisor + both `grpcurl` paths) ran end-to-end on the slow-clock armhf DUT.

Hey @yxieca
In J2C params, we don't have a specified packet size for xon/xoff profiles, we add it for the headroompoolsize test specifically. The current PR follows the same for 120KM profile. There is a followup PR in works which follows the same parameter tuning pattern for both 30m and 120KM along with a decided packet size.
Currently bufferpool watermark isn't supported on DNX, hence the check.
Will add the TODO.

@wangxin — re-reviewed at head `e049257`. ✅ **Approve.** My original blocker is fully resolved, and in a way that is strictly better than the gated rewrite I'd suggested. Thanks for the thoughtful iteration with @saiarcot895 and @lolyu.

### Original blocker (qemu.conf unconditional rewrite) — fully resolved ✅

The previous unconditional `user/group = "root"` rewrite of `/etc/libvirt/qemu.conf` is **gone entirely**. The new approach is:

- No modification of `/etc/libvirt/qemu.conf` anywhere in the role. QEMU keeps running as the unprivileged `libvirt-qemu` user on both 22.04 and 24.04 — distro security model preserved.
- The actual root cause (Ubuntu's newer `/etc/adduser.conf` setting `DIR_MODE=0750`, blocking `libvirt-qemu` from traversing `/home/<user>` to reach `~/sonic-vm/disks/`) is fixed with a targeted POSIX ACL in `ansible/roles/vm_set/tasks/main.yml:163-171`:
  ```yaml
  - name: Allow libvirt-qemu to traverse home directory
    acl:
      path: "{{ home_path }}"
      entity: libvirt-qemu
      etype: user
      permissions: x       # traverse only, not r-x
      state: present
    become: yes
    when: package_installation | bool
  ```
- `acl` package added to the apt list in `host_setup.yml:27` — required by the `acl` Ansible module.
- No libvirtd restart, no `force_qemu_root` flag, no version split. Same code path on 22.04 and 24.04. ACL grants traverse-only (`--x`), survives `chmod`, and mask resolves to `r-x` so no shrinking of existing group perms.
- PR description's "Caveat for hosts that previously ran an earlier iteration" gives the manual `sed`+`systemctl` rollback for testers who already have the broken `qemu.conf`. 

This is the cleanest landing of the three iterations — thanks for working through it.

### Smaller follow-ups from my prior review

| Concern | Status |
|---|---|
| Idempotent `uv pip install` on every play invocation | ✅ Addressed. `ensure_python_env.yml:34-50` pre-checks `requests` + `docker` versions and skips `uv pip install` when both match `['2.33.1', '7.1.0']`. `control_mux_simulator.yml:19-35` does the same for Flask/Werkzeug. |
| `connection_db` still using global pip | Withdraw — not applicable to this role (I was conflating with another change). |
| Unpinned `grpcio` | 🟡 **Still unpinned** in `control_nic_simulator.yml:35-42` (`uv pip install grpcio grpcio-tools` with no `==`), and the presence-check at L29-33 only does `import grpc, grpc_tools` (not a version check). Means: any drift in installed gRPC vs. what `nic_simulator` expects won't trigger a reinstall, and a future grpcio CVE bump won't propagate without manual intervention. **Non-blocking, but worth a small follow-up PR** to pin versions (similar to how `requests==2.33.1 docker==7.1.0` are pinned in `ensure_python_env.yml`) and gate reinstall on `stdout_lines` matching the pin. |

### New minor observations (all non-blocking, mention for future polish)

1. **`force_host_setup` env lookup is unconditional** (`main.yml:58-60`) — does not respect a pre-set playbook variable the way `package_installation` does (L48-56). Asymmetric.
2. **`/usr/bin/env` wrapper is redundant** in both `mux-simulator.service.j2:6` and `nic-simulator.service.j2:6` — `{{ sonic_testbed_venv }}/bin/python3` is already an absolute path, so `env` adds no value (also costs an extra `execve`). Pre-existing pattern in the templates, just calling it out.
3. **`docker.yml:39-52`** unconditionally runs `apt-get update` + `apt install docker-ce` even when the docker repo already exists. Harmless (apt is idempotent) and only runs inside the once-per-host `host_setup` gate. Could be wrapped under the same `docker_repo.matched == 0` block.
4. **`host_setup_version: "1"`** (`main.yml:65`) is a manual bump string — fine as long as the inline comment at L62 stays prominent so future contributors actually bump it when adding sysctls/packages.
5. Verified the `slurp` of `/opt/sonic-testbed/.host_setup_done` with `ignore_errors: yes` and the `default('')` fallback (L72-84) is safe — the Jinja `or` chain short-circuits on `not host_setup_flag.stat.exists`, so the unknown-content case is handled.

### Verdict

✅ **Approve.** Shipping. The POSIX-ACL approach is meaningfully better than any flavor of `qemu.conf` rewrite, gated or not. The only follow-up I'd encourage in a separate PR is **pinning `grpcio` / `grpcio-tools`** in `control_nic_simulator.yml` for reproducibility and CVE-response posture.

For 22.04 users who hit libvirt disk-image permission errors, there's now an opt-in variable `force_qemu_root` that can be set to `true` to trigger the same fix. This avoids unexpected libvirtd restarts on existing 22.04 testbeds.

> @StormLiangMS Thanks for the review! Agreed on the qemu.conf concern — I've re-gated it to only apply on Ubuntu >= 24.04. For 22.04 users who hit libvirt disk-image permission errors, there's now an opt-in variable `force_qemu_root` that can be set to `true` to trigger the same fix. Regarding this, I haven't seen this permission issue when I was using Ubuntu 24.04. I also don't think qemu (when started as libvirt) should be running as root when the intention of the distro (and maybe libvirt themselves) is to run it as a non-root user. Is this the best fix to make? What was the actual original issue?

Edit, from [this](https://developers.redhat.com/articles/2024/12/18/rootless-virtual-machines-kvm-and-qemu#session_vms_are_rootless-h2):

> Libvirt system VMs do not run as root. They run as the QEMU system user. They have only access to host resources that were previously set up by libvirt.

> System VMs require a number of helpers from libvirt, which run with elevated privileges, to attach to host storage and networking. That means system VMs are not entirely rootless. Those helpers reduce the attach surface of libvirt and avoid the need of granting root privileges directly to any QEMU process or VM process.

@saiarcot895 @StormLiangMS — wanted to consolidate the qemu.conf discussion since the approach has evolved meaningfully across iterations of this PR.

**Original symptom:** on freshly provisioned Ubuntu hosts (especially 24.04) the SONiC VM fails to boot with:

```
libvirt.libvirtError: Cannot access storage file
  ''/home/<user>/sonic-vm/disks/sonic_vlab-01.img'' (as uid:64055, gid:108): Permission denied
```

**Root cause** (which I had to actually verify rather than guess): `/etc/adduser.conf` on newer Ubuntu releases sets `DIR_MODE=0750` instead of `0755`. The unprivileged `libvirt-qemu` user is in groups `{kvm, libvirt-qemu}` — neither matches the user''s group on `/home/<user>` — so it cannot traverse the home directory to reach the disk image. Comparing two of my hosts confirmed this is the only difference: vm0 (created in 2024, `DIR_MODE=0755`) works out of the box; vm1 (created in 2026, `DIR_MODE=0750`) does not. No AppArmor denial is involved on 24.04 — the AppArmor `libvirt-<vm-uuid>` profile dynamically allows the configured disk path; it''s purely a filesystem traversal issue.

**What previous iterations did and why they were wrong:**

- *Iteration 1:* unconditionally rewrote `/etc/libvirt/qemu.conf` to run QEMU as root on Ubuntu 24.04+. @StormLiangMS correctly flagged that this would silently flip behavior on every existing 22.04 testbed too if they ever upgraded.
- *Iteration 2:* re-gated to `>= 24.04` and added a `force_qemu_root=true` opt-in flag for 22.04. @saiarcot895 correctly pushed back that running QEMU as root goes against the distro security model — libvirt explicitly designs system VMs to run as `libvirt-qemu` with a small set of privileged helpers, and root QEMU broadens the attack surface for what should be an unprivileged process.

**Final approach in this PR:** drop the qemu.conf rewrite and the `force_qemu_root` flag entirely. Grant the `libvirt-qemu` user the minimum permission it actually needs via a targeted POSIX ACL on `home_path`:

```yaml
- name: Allow libvirt-qemu to traverse home directory
  acl:
    path: "{{ home_path }}"
    entity: libvirt-qemu
    etype: user
    permissions: x
    state: present
```

Properties:
- QEMU continues to run as the unprivileged `libvirt-qemu` user — distro security model preserved.
- ACL grants `--x` (traverse) only, not `r-x`. `libvirt-qemu` cannot `ls` or read anything else in the home directory.
- Mask resolves to `r-x`, so existing group permissions are not silently shrunk.
- Survives subsequent `chmod` operations on the home dir.
- Same code path works on Ubuntu 22.04 and 24.04 — no version gates.
- No libvirtd restart needed.
- Other users on the host are unaffected; each user''s home gets its own scoped ACL.
- On hosts where `DIR_MODE=0755` (e.g. older Ubuntu releases) the ACL is redundant-but-harmless — the world bit was already sufficient for traversal.

**Verified end-to-end** with `testbed-cli.sh add-topo vms-kvm-t0` on both Ubuntu 22.04 and Ubuntu 24.04. In both cases `qemu.conf` is left at its libvirt defaults, the QEMU process runs as `libvirt-qemu`, and `sudo -u libvirt-qemu test -r /home/<user>/sonic-vm/disks/sonic_vlab-01.img` returns 0.

One caveat (also called out in the updated PR description): hosts that ran an earlier iteration of this branch will still have `user = "root"` in their `qemu.conf`. The role no longer touches that file, so the stale state persists. A one-time `sed` + `systemctl restart libvirtd` reverts it. Given that no one outside this PR review thread has run those earlier iterations, the impact should be minimal.

Thanks to both of you for pushing back on the original approach — the final result is meaningfully better than where this started.

### TL;DR

I think this PR over-prunes. The diff doesn't just drop "validate routes received from BGPS" — it deletes the entire **community-driven advertisement-control** assertion, which is the actual feature `test_bgp_sentinel` was designed to validate. After this PR, `test_bgp_sentinel` is effectively "iBGP session reset and re-establishment + DUT advertises ≥1 route somewhere", which is a generic BGP smoke test, not a sentinel test.

I'd ask either to (a) restore the no-export / community semantics check (perhaps minus the receive-side validation if that's the part that's no longer accurate), or (b) clarify in the PR description which other PR/test is taking over the sentinel-specific coverage.

### Functional concerns

**1. The test no longer validates the BGP-sentinel feature.** Before this PR, `prepare_bgp_sentinel_routes` was parametrized over `bgp_community` ∈ {`sentinel_community`, `sentinel_community + no-export`}. The post-announce assertion was the meaningful one:

```python
if 'no-export' in community:
    pytest_assert(
        not is_route_advertised_to_ebgp_peers(...),
        "Route {} should not be advertised to bgp peers".format(route),
    )
else:
    pytest_assert(
        is_route_advertised_to_ebgp_peers(...),
        ...
    )
```

That asserts the DUT **honors no-export from sentinel-injected paths** — the entire point of sentinel. After this PR, the only remaining assertion (`check_routes_advertised_to_ibgp_peers`) checks `len(advertised) > 0` to each iBGP session. That's true for any working router/peer pair regardless of whether sentinel is even installed.

The PR description rationale — "DUT does not need to receive route from BGPS service" — only justifies dropping the *received-routes-from-PTF* validation, not the announce/withdraw + community-override flow. If the sentinel feature has truly changed so that DUT no longer receives routes from PTF, please link the SONiC repo PR / spec change so reviewers can verify, and please add a positive assertion that exercises whatever the new sentinel contract is.

**2. The PR title and description are misleading.** Title says "remove route validation from BGPS service"; the diff actually removes:
- `is_route_advertised_to_ebgp_peers` (advertisement-side check)
- `change_route` / `announce_route` / `withdraw_route` (PTF announce/withdraw)
- `get_target_routes` (target route discovery)
- `sentinel_community` / `bgp_community` fixtures (community parametrization)
- The IPv4/IPv6 route-direction parametrization
- The whole no-export semantics validation

Either retitle the PR or split into multiple commits whose titles match what each removes.

**3. `check_routes_advertised_to_ibgp_peers` only checks `len(advertised) > 0`.** This is a surprisingly weak assertion. If you wanted to keep the assertion lightweight, comparing the per-peer count against the count taken before the reset would at least catch "advertisement leak after reset" regressions — the current form passes as long as at least one route is still advertised.

**4. After reset, no per-peer wait — only `time.sleep(10)`.** The session-up wait uses `wait_until(30, 5, 5, is_bgp_sentinel_session_established, ...)` which is good, but the *advertised-routes* are then checked after a hardcoded 10-second sleep. On a busy/slow KVM testbed this can flake on hard reset. Consider replacing the bare `time.sleep(10)` with a `wait_until` poll over the advertised-routes count to make it deterministic.

### Code-level nits

**5. `peer_addr = ipaddress.ip_address(peer.encode().decode())`** — the `.encode().decode()` is a no-op on a `str` (UTF-8 round-trip). Just `ipaddress.ip_address(peer)`. Same pattern was present in the deleted code; please drop it on the way through.

**6. Unused unpacked variables.** The new test body unpacks 8 values from `common_setup_teardown` but only uses `ibgp_sessions` (and `tbinfo` from a separate fixture). Replace unused names with `_` to make it obvious nothing else is needed:

```python
_, _, _, _, _, ibgp_sessions, _, _ = common_setup_teardown
```

If `common_setup_teardown` is now over-providing, that's a separate cleanup but worth at least flagging in a TODO.

**7. `reset_type == "none"` branch is a no-op.** It runs `check_routes_advertised_to_ibgp_peers` once and returns. That's the same effect as removing the parametrization for "none" entirely — the parametrize set `["none", "soft", "hard"]` adds a third case that contributes nothing the un-parametrized first call already covered. Either drop "none" or use it to assert something distinct (e.g., counts equal across cases).

**8. The for-loop applies clear semantics per-peer in sequence.** On `hard` reset of multiple sessions, all are cleared back-to-back before the wait. The `wait_until(30, 5, 5, ...)` then waits up to 30s for **all** sessions to come up — fine, but worth knowing throughput drops to 0 across the board momentarily. If sessions matter for traffic-side asserts elsewhere, may want to clear-and-wait per-peer.

**9. DCO check is failing.** Commit message has no `Signed-off-by:` line. Please amend with `git commit --amend -s` and force-push.

**10. PR description has mojibake** — "ΓÇª" is the CP437 misrendering of UTF-8 ellipsis "…". Same in the commit message headline ("BGPΓÇª"). Cosmetic but visible in `gh pr list`/changelog.

### Things I like

- Function naming is much clearer (`check_routes_advertised_to_ibgp_peers` is a more accurate name than the old `is_route_advertised_to_ebgp_peers`).
- Removing `requests` and `yaml` imports + dead helpers is good housekeeping.
- The `reset_type` parametrization shape (`none`/`soft`/`hard`) is a nice replacement for the old per-fixture branching, IF you keep something meaningful in the body for each branch.

### Summary recommendation

**Request changes** — primarily on item #1 (lost coverage of community/no-export semantics). The community-driven advertisement control IS the BGP sentinel feature; if this test no longer needs to assert it, the PR description should say *why* (link to the product change that moved that responsibility elsewhere, or to the new test that takes it over). The other items (#2–#10) are smaller fixes that should land in the same PR.

@guangyao6 please take a look — happy to discuss any of the above.

Hello @StormLiangMS ：
For #1, As dut do not receive routes from bgps service, so there is no need to test bgps service announce/withdraw routes to dut, and also with the route validation part. Now bgp sentinel service will only receive routes from dut, not advertise routes to dut.
For #2, changed the title as "Refactor BGP sentinel test to as BGPS Service do not send route to dut", so the functions listed in this part are not needed.
For #3, check the routes send to bgps service is enough for the test, as the route-map from dut to bgps service may change, so only check the ability for dut send bgp route to bgps service is enough here.
For #4, the wait_until is for is_bgp_sentinel_session_established, and when session established, wait for 10 seconds for sending bgp route information. This part should be kept, no need to replace with wait_until to check the routes advertised.
For #5, there is no need to delete `.encode().decode()`, This is used for # noqa: F841, many code has the same pattern.
For #6, fixed with TODO as there is plan to change the route-map to BGPS service, and will remove the parameters at that time.
For #7, will keep it as it is, as None represent the original state, and soft and hard represent the session reset operation. So combine them together as no operation, soft, hard.
For #8, do not need clear-and-wait per-peer, as the ibgp session are used for BGPS service, others are ebgp session, and is_bgp_sentinel_session_established will wait for all sessions to establish.

Thanks for the review @Xichen96 ! Responses inline:

**1. Branch name vs. file edited:**
The branch name `dev/xuliping/20260507_7060_fanout_202511_support` refers to the target branch this support is intended for (202511), we use this branch for verification, not the template file being modified. The change is adding 7060CX fanout support to `sonic_deploy_202205.j2` so it can be used as the base template for the fanout which is running 202205 image. The naming is intentional — it reflects the feature goal, not the file changed.

**2. `in ("Arista-7060CX-32S-C32")` style:**
This is intentional and consistent with the existing pattern already used in [`sonic_deploy_202012.j2`](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-mgmt/blob/master/ansible/roles/fanout/templates/sonic_deploy_202012.j2), which uses the same `in ("single_string")` form in multiple places — e.g., `fanout_hwsku in ("Arista-7060CX-32S-C32")` and `fanout_hwsku not in ("Celestica-E1031-T48S4")`. While technically a substring check in Python/Jinja2, it produces the correct result since HWSKU values are never substrings of each other. Changing this PR alone would introduce inconsistency with the surrounding templates. Happy to do a codebase-wide cleanup in a separate PR if preferred.

Why this PR only adds PHY (not all 6 types like the master PR #24026):

On the 202511 branch, `verify_counterpoll_status` in `test_counterpoll_watermark.py` uses substring matching ([`if k in counterpoll_parsed_dict[TYPE]`](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-mgmt/blob/c0236c9970ee3bcb65a1874e1ed146143eb9c69f/tests/platform_tests/counterpoll/test_counterpoll_watermark.py#L249)), while master has already been updated to use exact matching ([`==`](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-mgmt/blob/ee58edbcb00d38ec7fb41938d9abecc1e673b03a/tests/platform_tests/counterpoll/test_counterpoll_watermark.py#L281)).

This causes a problem: when `WRED_ECN_QUEUE_STAT` appears in counterpoll show output, `'QUEUE_STAT' in 'WRED_ECN_QUEUE_STAT'` matches as True, causing the queue status to be incorrectly overwritten by wredqueue
  's status. This leads to `queue is disable. expected to be enable` failures on KVM.

PHY doesn't have this issue because it's not a substring of any other type name.

The master PR passed because master already replaced substring matching (`in`) with exact matching (`==`) in verify_counterpoll_status. Once that fix is backported to 202511, the remaining types can be safely added.

@opcoder0 thanks for the work — sharing review notes below. Nothing posted as inline review threads; this is a single summary comment so feel free to triage / push back as needed.

## 🔴 Bugs / likely runtime failures

### 1. `Date` GraphQL fields will reject ISO-8601 timestamps (high impact)
`reporting._update_generic_field` routes anything with `dataType == "DATE"` through `_update_date_field`, which sends the value as `Date!`. The `fields` dict passes raw GitHub timestamps like `2026-04-27T06:44:08Z` for `issue_created_at`, `issue_closed_at`, `last_updated_at`, and `last_comment_at`. GitHub's GraphQL `Date` scalar requires `YYYY-MM-DD` and will return a GraphQL error → the only retry is "once", so the upsert path will spam errors at runtime. Either truncate to `.split("T")[0]` before sending, or define those project columns as Text. Only `expiry_date` is currently safe.

### 2. `logging.warning` arity mismatch
`cli.py` (post-merge):
```python
logging.getLogger(__name__).warning(
    "Skipping mutation for %d cross-repo issue(s); they are still included in reporting",
    len(skipped_issues),
    args.target_repo,   # ← extra arg, format string only has %d
)
```
Raises `TypeError: not all arguments converted during string formatting` inside the logging machinery (printed to stderr, message not emitted). Was 2 args / 2 placeholders pre-PR.

### 3. `remove_label` regression — bypasses retries entirely
The simplified `_request` lost the `success_statuses={404}` parameter, so `remove_label` now calls `self.session.delete(...)` directly. That bypasses *all* retry, *all* backoff, *all* error logging from the centralized retry path. A transient 5xx or connection blip on label removal will fail the whole run, where previously it would retry. This is a real correctness regression caused by simplifying `_request`.

### 4. GitHub REST client lost capabilities (off-scope refactor regression)
The `github_api.py` rewrite removes:
- 403 + `X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0` retry handling — now treated as a hard failure.
- `max_backoff_seconds` cap — backoff grows unbounded (`backoff_factor * 2^attempt`).
- `Retry-After` parsing tolerance — the new code does `float(headers.get("Retry-After", default))` directly. If GitHub returns an HTTP-date instead of seconds (allowed by RFC), `float()` raises and aborts.

This refactor is unrelated to "Project V2 reporting" and reduces robustness. **Suggest splitting it into a separate PR or reverting** — the existing tests in `unit_test_github_api_retry.py` cover only the simple cases that still pass and won't catch any of these.

## 🟠 Functional issues

### 5. `--no-op` does NOT cover the project board
`SkipExpiryManager` honors `--no-op`, but the reporter only honors `SKIP_EXPIRY_REPORT_DRY_RUN`. The workflow YAML wires them together via `github.event.inputs.no_op`, so CI is fine, **but anyone running locally with `--no-op` will still mutate the Project V2**. The CLI flag should also force `dry_run=True` on the reporter.

### 6. `_reporting_auth_env` context manager is mostly dead/confusing
The reporter authenticates with `self.token` (captured at construction). The CLI still wraps every `reporter.upsert_project_item` and `reporter.summary()` call in `_reporting_auth_env`, which mutates `os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"]`. The reporter never reads `GITHUB_TOKEN` after init. Wrapping the construction call inside `create_reporter_from_env()` is the only one that matters; all the per-row wraps can be removed. This also avoids the (small) chance of leaking the App token if other code unexpectedly reads `GITHUB_TOKEN` mid-loop.

### 7. Dead code added to `conditional_marks.py`
`collect_issue_test_mapping_from_conditional_marks` and its helper `_extract_test_issue_entries` are introduced by this PR but **never called** from anywhere in the new code path (which uses `collect_report_entries_from_conditional_marks`). Drop them, or call them from somewhere.
Also: the old `collect_github_issues_from_conditional_marks` is no longer used by `cli.py` either — keep it only if external code depends on it.

### 8. Performance / API budget — every upsert rewrites every field
- `update_project_item` issues one mutation per field (~30) plus one create per row; for N rows, that's `O(30·N)` mutations every workflow run, regardless of whether anything changed.
- `fetch_project_items` only requests `ProjectV2ItemFieldTextValue`, so existing values cannot be diffed against the new desired values — no way to skip no-op writes.
- `graphql_request` retries **once** for any failure, no 429/Retry-After honoring, no 5xx exponential backoff.

You will hit secondary rate limits on a real-sized project very quickly. At minimum, honor `Retry-After` and 5xx; ideally compare current vs. desired and only mutate diffs.

### 9. `fieldValues(first: 100)` not paginated
Items are paginated correctly, but each item's `fieldValues` is capped at 100. Once a row has >100 fields the test_id detection silently fails for that row → a duplicate row gets created on the next run.

### 10. `expiry_bucket` boundary labels are off-by-one
```python
if days_to_expiry <= 1: "0-1d"
if days_to_expiry <= 7: "1-7d"   # actually covers 2..7
if days_to_expiry <= 15: "7-15d" # actually covers 8..15
```
Buckets overlap by name and don't cover their advertised range. Either rename (`"≤1d"`, `"2-7d"`, `"8-15d"`, `"16-30d"`, `">30d"`) or fix boundaries.

### 11. `current_status` value naming is inconsistent
Possible values: `"expired"`, `"not expired"` (with a space), `"skip_closed"`, `"no_issue_linked"`, `"unknown"`. The single-select option lookup is case-insensitive but space-sensitive. Pick one convention (recommend `not_expired`) so the project columns stay sane.

### 12. `evaluate_issue` skips expiry calculation for closed issues
For a closed issue, `evaluation` is returned with `created_at=None`, `expiry_at=None`. Result: closed-issue rows always show `expiry_date=""` even though the issue's `created_at` is known and the configured `expiry_days` is known. If you want the historical expiry date in the report, compute it for closed issues too.

### 13. `_normalize_condition_file` may not normalize
`conditional_marks.py` passes `relative_file = str(mark_file)` (whatever path glob returned), and `cli._normalize_condition_file` then does `Path(...).resolve().relative_to(repo_root)`. If `mark_file` is already a relative path and CWD isn't the repo root (entirely possible for App-token workflows), `resolve()` will produce a path outside `repo_root` and silently fall back to the unresolved string. Pass an absolute path from `conditional_marks.py` (`mark_file.resolve()`).

### 14. Workflow always provisions an App token
The `Get GitHub App token` step runs unconditionally. If `GH_APP_ID` / `GH_APP_PRIVATE_KEY` aren't configured (e.g., on a fork running this workflow), the step will fail before reporting could have been disabled. Gate on a repo variable like `ENABLE_REPORTING` (workflow `if`-on-secret is restricted).

## 🟡 Quality / nits

- **No tests added.** The PR introduces ~650 lines of `reporting.py` and substantial CLI/parser logic. Zero new unit tests. The PR description's "Add unit tests" refers to a previous commit, but those tests target the old retry surface that this PR is now changing. The Skip Expiry Unit Tests CI check (still passing) doesn't exercise any new code.
- **Pin `actions/create-github-app-token`** to a SHA (security advice for community actions).
- **Duplicate work:** `source_repo = f"{_normalize_repo_name(args.target_repo)[0]}/{_normalize_repo_name(args.target_repo)[1]}"` calls the parser twice and just lowercases `args.target_repo`. Replace with one call or just `args.target_repo.lower()`.
- **`needs_cleanup`** is fully redundant with `current_status == "skip_closed"`; same data in two columns.
- **`_extract_test_id` falls back to title** — slight risk of accidentally adopting a hand-created draft issue if its title happens to look like a test_id. Add a defensive note or an explicit prefix/marker on the title.
- **No deletion / staleness handling.** When a YAML mark is removed, the Project V2 row lingers forever. Either document this as expected, or add a "needs_removal" flag for rows whose `test_id` no longer appears in the latest scan.
- **`age_days` rounding:** `int(negative // 86400)` floors toward −∞, so an issue created 10.5 days ago reports `age_days = 11`. `days_to_expiry` rounds the other way (toward 0). Pick one rounding mode for consistency.
- **`_derive_test_category`**: returns the leading directory (e.g. `tests`) for paths like `tests/route/test_x.py`. If the YAML keys actually start with `tests/`, every category collapses to `"tests"` and `maintainer_map` becomes useless. Worth documenting and/or stripping a known prefix.
- **`field_map` keyed by `name.lower()`**: case collisions on user-defined field names silently drop one. Log a warning when overwriting.

## ✅ Things I liked

- Clear separation: `evaluate_issue` (read-only) vs `process_issue` (mutating).
- Cross-repo issues now still appear in the report — good UX win.
- Single `dataclass TestReportData` keeps the row schema discoverable.
- `is_permanent_skip` semantics (skip with no linked issue) are well chosen.
- Idempotent upsert via `existing_items` cache is the right design.
- App-token usage with proper environment fallbacks.

## TL;DR — top 5 to fix before merge

1. **#1 Date scalar** — will fail at runtime for 4 fields.
2. **#3 `remove_label` retry bypass** + **#4 GitHub REST regressions** — split out or revert the `github_api.py` refactor.
3. **#2 logging arity bug** — small fix, but emits errors on every cross-repo run.
4. **#5 `--no-op` doesn't cover the project board** — surprise mutations from local runs.
5. **#8 API budget** — at minimum, add 429/Retry-After/5xx handling to `graphql_request`; ideally diff before writing.

Drop them, or call them from somewhere. Also: the old `collect_github_issues_from_conditional_marks` is no longer used by `cli.py` either — keep it only if external code depends on it.
> 
> ### 9. `fieldValues(first: 100)` not paginated

If you want the historical expiry date in the report, compute it for closed issues too.

  - (12) closed issues will be shown in the report under a Needs Cleanup report which would indicate those issues have been closed and needs to be cleaned up from conditional mark. So, there won't be a need to age them.

@opcoder0 — Re-reviewed against `f10ac74`. Massive improvement, thanks for the thorough turnaround. Almost all of my v1 findings are addressed (date scalar, logging arity, `remove_label` retry, REST safeguards, `--no-op` covering the project board, redundant auth-env wraps, off-by-one bucket labels, status naming, and the upsert API budget via the new diff cache + GraphQL retry hardening). The new unit test scaffold is a nice bonus.

Two items I'd like to see before approving — only one is a real blocker:

### 🔴 Blocker — `github_api.py` 403 handler can crash the workflow

In the restored 403 + `X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0` branch:

```python
reset_time = response.headers.get("X-RateLimit-Reset")
sleep_secs = float(reset_time) - time.time() if reset_time else self._cap_backoff(...)
time.sleep(sleep_secs)
```

- If `X-RateLimit-Reset` is in the past (clock skew, stale reset on a re-issued 403, or simply the reset already fired between the headers being written and our parse), `sleep_secs` is negative → `time.sleep()` raises `ValueError` and the workflow run dies.
- If the value is far in the future, we sleep unbounded (no `max_backoff_seconds` cap, unlike the `else` branch).
- `float(reset_time)` will also raise if the header is malformed.

Suggested 1-liner (plus a `try`):

```python
try:
    raw = float(reset_time) if reset_time else None
except ValueError:
    raw = None
sleep_secs = self._cap_backoff(...) if raw is None else max(0.0, min(self.max_backoff_seconds, raw - time.time()))
time.sleep(sleep_secs)
```

### 🟠 Strongly suggested — `ProjectV2Reporter._extract_test_id` title fallback can hijack rows

`reporting.py`:

```python
def _extract_test_id(self, item):
    ...
    title = ((((item.get("content") or {}).get("title")) or "").strip())
    return title
```

Any draft issue someone adds to the project by hand, whose title happens to look like a test path, becomes "ours" — `update_project_item` will then overwrite its fields on every run. We should treat missing `test_id` as "not ours":

```python
return ""  # only rows that opted in via the test_id text field are managed
```

If you want a one-time backfill for legacy rows, gate it behind an explicit flag.

---

### Non-blocking — fine to land in a follow-up

These are all minor; happy to defer.

- **F-07** Dead code in `conditional_marks.py`: `_extract_test_issue_entries` and `collect_issue_test_mapping_from_conditional_marks` are still unreferenced. Wire them up or delete.
- **F-09** `fieldValues(first: 100)` in `fetch_project_items` isn't paginated. Safe today (project has <30 fields) but will silently overwrite once it grows. At minimum, assert `len(nodes) < 100` so it fails loudly.
- **F-12** `evaluate_issue` returns `expiry_at=None` for closed issues, but the new `skip-closed` status implies we want the timing fields. Either compute them anyway or document the omission.
- **F-14** Workflow YAML still provisions the App token unconditionally. Gate with `if: env.PROJECT_ID != ''` or similar.
- **F-N2** `_parse_retry_after` is inconsistent — `reporting.py` parses HTTP-date via `email.utils.parsedate_to_datetime` (good), `github_api.py` only does `float(header)` and falls back. Promote the better implementation to a shared helper.
- **F-N3** `_update_single_select_field` clears the field when no matching option, but `_update_current_status_field` falls back to text. Pick one strategy and document that maintainers must keep SINGLE_SELECT options aligned with the constants in `cli.py`.
- **F-N4** Diff cache stores SINGLE_SELECT names verbatim (e.g. `"Expired"`); `_normalize_outgoing_value` only lowercases booleans. Result: every run re-issues the same option write. Normalise cached SINGLE_SELECT values via `.strip().lower()`, or compare option_id rather than display name.
- **F-N6** New `unit_test_reporting_basic.py` covers the helpers nicely but skips the upsert/diff happy path, the HTTP-date `Retry-After` branch, and `fieldValues > 100`. Good follow-up scope.
- **F-N7** `cli.py` L363 still calls `_normalize_repo_name(args.target_repo)` twice — hoist into a local.
- **F-N8** `_truncate_timestamp_to_date` is in `cli.py`, but it exists solely for the GraphQL Date scalar. Consider moving into `reporting.py` so the cli layer just hands raw timestamps in.

---

### Verdict

Once **F-N1** (and ideally **F-N5**) is addressed, I'm happy to approve. The rest can ship in a follow-up. Thanks again for the detailed iteration!

## Pre-check fixes (commit `5f3603720`)

Single commit on top of the original PR (#24354 first commit `19528aa19`) that resolves the two CI failures introduced by master's stricter pre-checks on new files:

### 1. flake8 E114/E116 on `egress_drop_probing.py:205`

The continuation comment after `add_flow(...)` had a non-multiple-of-4 indent. Moved to a block comment above the call.

### 2. CodeQL `py/call/wrong-arguments` on `egress_drop_probing_executor.py`

The repo defines `sai_thrift_read_port_counters` twice with different arities:

| File | Signature | Used at runtime? |
|---|---|---|
| `tests/saitests/switch.py:751` | `(client, port)` (2 args, py2 legacy) | No |
| `tests/saitests/py3/switch.py:799` | `(client, asic_type, port)` (3 args, py3) | **Yes** (PTF runs py3) |

PTF resolves to the 3-arg version at runtime via `sys.path` manipulation. CodeQL static analysis binds to the 2-arg legacy version and flags every 3-arg call. Master sibling executors (`pfc_xoff_probing_executor.py`, `ingress_drop_probing_executor.py`) and `py3/sai_qos_tests.py` share the same pattern with 100+ pre-existing alerts on master — the rule is structurally noisy on this codebase, but new files added by a PR trigger NEW alerts that block CI.

**Fix**: localized pass-through wrapper inside the new file:

```python
def _sai_thrift_read_port_counters(*args, **kwargs):
    """Pass-through call to ``sai_thrift_read_port_counters``..."""
    return sai_thrift_read_port_counters(*args, **kwargs)
```

The `*args/**kwargs` signature breaks CodeQL's arity check; runtime semantics are identical (positional/keyword args pass through transparently). The `_` prefix on the same name keeps the relationship to the underlying helper visible.

### Why this approach

Tested 11 alternatives locally with `codeql-cli 2.25.3` + `codeql/python-queries 1.8.1`:

- 7 inline-comment variants (`# lgtm`, `# Suppression:`, `# noqa`, `# codeql[...]`, etc.) — all FAIL (this repo's CodeQL config doesn't honor in-source suppression)
- `_X = X` assignment alias — FAIL (CodeQL data flow follows it)
- `from M import X as _X` import alias — FAIL (same)
- `*args/**kwargs` wrapper — **PASS** ✓
- `query-filters: exclude` in codeql-config.yml — would also work but touches security config (not appropriate for this scoped fix)

### Diff

```
.github/codeql/codeql-config.yml                       | unchanged
tests/saitests/probe/egress_drop_probing.py            | +3 -2  (block comment)
tests/saitests/probe/egress_drop_probing_executor.py   | +21 -2 (wrapper + call sites)
```

No changes outside the probe new file scope.

### Verification

- Local CodeQL CLI on `egress_drop_probing_executor.py`: **0 alerts** (was 2)
- Mock UT (`test_egress_drop_probing`): **28 passed**
- Mock IT (`test_egress_drop_probing`): **23 passed**
- flake8: clean

## CI failure addressed via new commit

Pushed 7dd77f14d to fix the failing check `Probe UT/IT — Run Probe UT/IT`.

**Root cause** (pre-existing on master, surfaced for the first time by this PR's new CI step):

`tests/saitests/mock/ut/test_probing_base.py::TestProbingBaseGetRxPort::test_get_rx_port_wrapper`
asserted `mock_log.call_count == 2` (before+after via `log_message`). But `probing_base.get_rx_port()` was refactored at some point to:
- emit **one** `log_message` call (the dst/src port summary), and
- route the per-attempt before/after logs through `ProbingObserver.trace`.

The UT had been silently broken whenever someone ran mock UT directly — but no CI ran the mock UT, so it went unnoticed.

**Fix** (32-line surgical change to a single test method):
- Patch `probing_observer.ProbingObserver.trace` alongside `probing_base.log_message`.
- Update assertions: `mock_log.call_count == 1` and `mock_trace.call_count >= 2`.

This is a nice demonstration that the new probe-tests CI stage is doing its job — it caught a real pre-existing UT vs production drift on its first run.

**Verification**:
`pytest test_probing_base.py::TestProbingBaseGetRxPort::test_get_rx_port_wrapper -v` → PASSED

Re-running CI now.

Thank you @yxieca  for pointing out the longer test runtime potentially. I have removed the backoff retry sleep entirely and replaced it with a max wait timeout that is more generous and flexible than the hard coded sleep. The motivation for the retries was to give it more time to create a successful console connection. The console connection is not mandatory for testing.

Before this PR, the console connection is established and retried asynchronously. Reboot command is then issued 5 seconds after. It's possible that a console connection fails to start during this time. Now that this part is synchronous with a max wait timeout, no console connection (and no cleanup path) would be attempted after reboot command is issued. So, the retry backoff logic is no longer required. I also tested this by running the 3 test suites.

apply_mirror_config() varies quite a bit between master and 202511.

apply_mirror_config() is for ERSPAN and it has to pass this required field.

@vvolam can you please take a look at this ?

> ### Description of PR
> Summary:
> 
> This PR enhances the SmartSwitch DPU platform test suite with two improvements:
> 
> DPU uptime-based reboot verification — After every reboot/reload test, each DPU's boot time is recorded before the operation and compared after recovery. This confirms DPUs actually rebooted, not just came back online (which could mask a case where the DPU never went down).
> 
> Syslog error checking post-reboot — After each reboot test, DPU and NPU syslogs are scanned for unexpected ERROR/CRIT messages. Known/expected patterns (e.g. transceiver errors, SAI NOT_FOUND) are filtered out. DPU syslog failures are hard assertions; NPU syslog failures are non-fatal warnings to avoid flakiness from unrelated system events.
> 
> ### Type of change
> * [ ]  Bug fix
> * [ ]  Testbed and Framework(new/improvement)
> * [ ]  New Test case
>   
>   * [ ]  Skipped for non-supported platforms
> * [ X] Test case improvement
> 
> ### Back port request
> * [ ]  202205
> * [ ]  202305
> * [ ]  202311
> * [ ]  202405
> * [ ]  202411
> * [ ]  202505
> * [ X] 202511
> 
> ### Approach
> #### What is the motivation for this PR?
> Existing reboot/reload tests only verified that DPUs came back online and were pingable after an NPU reboot. They did not verify:
> 
> That DPUs actually rebooted (boot time could be unchanged if the DPU never went down and came back cleanly) That there are no unexpected syslog errors on DPUs or the NPU after recovery This PR closes both gaps without introducing new test cases, by extending existing tests.
> 
> #### How did you do it?
> * Added DPU_SYSLOG_ERROR_IGNORE_PATTERNS — list of known/expected error patterns to suppress
> * Added get_dpu_uptime(dpuhosts, dpu_id) — retrieves DPU boot time via uptime -s
> * Added get_all_dpu_uptimes(dpuhosts, dpu_on_list) — collects pre-reboot boot times for all DPUs
> * Added verify_dpu_rebooted(dpuhosts, dpu_name, pre_boot_time) — compares pre/post boot times
> * Added verify_all_dpus_rebooted(dpuhosts, dpu_on_list, pre_boot_times) — bulk verification with assertion
> * Added check_dpu_syslog_errors(dpuhosts, dpu_id, since_boot_time) — scans DPU journalctl for errors
> * Added check_all_dpus_no_syslog_errors(dpuhosts, dpu_on_list, pre_boot_times) — asserts no errors on all DPUs
> * Added check_npu_syslog_errors(duthost, since_boot_time) — scans NPU journalctl for errors (returns list, non-asserting)
> * Updated post_test_dpu_check and post_test_dpus_check to accept optional pre_boot_times kwarg — when provided, uptime verification and syslog check are automatically performed per DPU
> 
> test_reload_dpu.py:
> 
> * All reboot/reload test cases now call get_all_dpu_uptimes() before the reboot operation and pass pre_boot_times to post_test_dpus_check()
> * test_dpu_status_post_switch_mem_exhaustion and test_dpu_status_post_switch_kernel_panic additionally check NPU syslog after recovery
> * test_cold_reboot_switch extended to also check NPU syslog after cold reboot
> * test_reboot_cause extended with pre_boot_times to verify DPUs actually rebooted during shutdown/startup cycle
> 
> #### How did you verify/test it?
> * Ran all modified test cases on a SmartSwitch testbed
> * Confirmed uptime comparison correctly detects rebooted vs non-rebooted DPUs
> * Confirmed syslog scan correctly suppresses known-noisy errors and surfaces genuine unexpected errors
> * Verified pre_boot_times=None (default) leaves existing behaviour unchanged for any callers not passing it
> 
> #### Any platform specific information?
> N/A
> 
> #### Supported testbed topology if it's a new test case?
> Smartswitch.
> 
> ### Documentation
> N/A

@wrideout-arista thanks for the backport — confirming this is needed for 202511 since the master version had conflicts.

One issue worth a fix before signoff:

**`ansible/roles/test/files/ptftests/py3/hash_test.py`, new v6 branch in `create_pkt` (~line 728):**

```python
exp_pkt['IPV6'].hlim -= 1
```

Should be `'IPv6'` (matching Scapy's class name and every other reference in this file, e.g. line 943, line 395). Scapy's `pkt[<name>]` lookup is case-sensitive against the class, so this will raise at runtime on v6-only topos. The existing KVM CI plans (t0/t1-lag/t2) aren't v6-only, so this code path isn't exercised in PR checks — which is exactly the configuration this PR targets.

Minor: please double-check `set_packet_parameter`'s `is_v6_topo` + inner `version='IP'` path — accessing `pkt['IPv6'].payload.proto` to set the inner-IPv4 protocol is correct in principle but worth verifying it's actually exercised on a v6 testbed.

Once the typo is fixed I'm happy to take another pass.

## Test Update & Results

### Changes in this update:
- **Removed `MIN_TIMEOUT=60s` floor** — the budget now scales purely from platform characteristics (`num_moves × loads_per_move × measured_loaddata_time × safety_multiplier`). The floor was masking real performance issues on platforms where `loadData` is fast.
- **Fixed cleanup** — uses direct ConfigDB deletion instead of apply-patch (which rejects patches that leave a table empty).

### KVM Testbed Results (32-port VS, master.1094504-eae46f301)

| Scenario | Time | Per port | loads/move | Budget | Result |
|----------|------|----------|------------|--------|--------|
| Baseline | 21.0s | 1.315s | 49.3x | 4s | ❌ FAIL |
| sonic-utilities [#4476](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-utilities/pull/4476) only | 21.1s | 1.321s | 45.3x | 5s | ❌ FAIL |
| sonic-utilities [#4478](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-utilities/pull/4478) only | 4.6s | 0.290s | 10.6x | 4s | ❌ FAIL (barely) |
| #4476 + #4478 combined | 4.6s | 0.284s | 7.7x | 6s | ✅ PASS |

The test correctly identifies the performance regression and validates that both sonic-utilities PRs are needed to pass.

## Test Update & Results

### Changes in this update:
- **Removed `MIN_TIMEOUT=60s` floor** — the budget now scales purely from platform characteristics. The floor was masking real performance issues on platforms where loadData is fast.
- **Fixed cleanup** — uses direct ConfigDB deletion instead of apply-patch (which rejects patches that leave a table empty).

### KVM Testbed Results (32-port VS, master.1094504-eae46f301)

| Scenario | Time | Per port | loads/move | Budget | Result |
|----------|------|----------|------------|--------|--------|
| Baseline | 21.0s | 1.315s | 49.3x | 4s | FAIL |
| sonic-utilities #4476 only | 21.1s | 1.321s | 45.3x | 5s | FAIL |
| sonic-utilities #4478 only | 4.6s | 0.290s | 10.6x | 4s | FAIL (barely) |
| #4476 + #4478 combined | 4.6s | 0.284s | 7.7x | 6s | PASS |

The test correctly identifies the performance regression and validates that both sonic-utilities PRs are needed to pass.

@rookie-who — reviewed `test_apply_patch_perf.py` end to end against the existing sonic-mgmt test patterns, `gu_utils.py`, and current GCU behavior. The test is useful and the platform-agnostic budget approach is the right design. However there are some issues that need to be addressed before merge. Please validate each point against the present code and behavior before making any changes — only fix what is actually broken.

### Bug 1 — `elapsed` and `per_port_time` can be undefined — `NameError` on failure

`elapsed` and `per_port_time` are assigned **inside** the `try` block but used **outside** it in the final `pytest_assert`:

```python
try:
    start_time = time.time()
    output = apply_patch(duthost, remove_patch, tmpfile)
    elapsed = time.time() - start_time        # ← only assigned if no exception
    per_port_time = elapsed / num_moves       # ← same
finally:
    delete_tmpfile(duthost, tmpfile)

# ... cleanup step 6 ...

pytest_assert(
    elapsed < budget,    # ← NameError if apply_patch raised TimeoutError
    "... per_port_time ...".format(elapsed, per_port_time, ...)
)
```

`gu_utils.apply_patch()` raises `TimeoutError` if the command exceeds `get_gcu_timeout()` (600s default). If that happens, both variables are never assigned and the final assert throws `NameError`, which completely masks the real failure. Initialize both to `None` before the `try` block and guard the final assert.

### Bug 2 — Missing `format_json_patch_for_multiasic()` — will fail on every multi-ASIC device

Every existing test in `tests/generic_config_updater/` calls `format_json_patch_for_multiasic()` before `apply_patch()`. Check `test_eth_interface.py`, `test_monitor_config.py` — they all do this:

```python
json_patch = format_json_patch_for_multiasic(duthost=duthost, json_data=json_patch,
                                             is_host_specific=True)
output = apply_patch(duthost, json_data=json_patch, dest_file=tmpfile)
```

This PR does not call it at all. On multi-ASIC platforms, the path `/ACL_TABLE/GCU_PERF_TEST` needs to be prefixed as `/localhost/ACL_TABLE/GCU_PERF_TEST`. Without it, all three patches — the add, the port removal, and the cleanup — will fail with `rc != 0` on every multi-ASIC DUT. Check the existing tests and apply the same pattern only if your platform targets require it.

### Bug 3 — `get_up_ports()` filters by `admin_status='up'` — returns 0 ports on VS/KVM

```python
up_ports = [name for name, cfg in ports.items()
            if cfg.get('admin_status', 'down') == 'up']
```

On VS and KVM testbeds — which are the exact platforms this test is meant to run on — ports are admin down by default. This returns 0 ports, fails `pytest_assert(num_ports >= MIN_TEST_PORTS)`, and the test skips entirely on the platform it was designed for. ACL table port bindings do not require ports to be admin up. Check `test_monitor_config.py`'s `get_valid_acl_ports` fixture for how existing tests handle this — it collects ports regardless of admin status.

### Behavior mismatch with PR #4478 — test assumption breaks after batching lands

The test comment says:

> "Uses a single REPLACE operation — GCU decomposes this into individual REMOVE moves internally"

This is true in current mainline but **not after PR #4478 lands**. `BulkLeafListMoveGenerator` in #4478 will emit a **single REPLACE** for `/ACL_TABLE/GCU_PERF_TEST/ports` — that is exactly the leaf-list this test targets. After #4478:
- GCU generates 1 move instead of N REMOVE moves
- `num_moves = len(ports_to_remove)` no longer reflects what GCU actually does
- The budget is still a ceiling so the test will pass, but it will **not catch regressions** in the per-item REMOVE path because that path is no longer exercised

Validate how GCU actually decomposes this patch against the current codebase. If #4478 is merged first, consider changing the patch to one that still forces many individual moves even after leaf-list batching (e.g., adding/removing individual ACL rules or entries that don't collapse into a single bulk REPLACE).

### If baseline measurement fails — skip, don't estimate

```python
# Fallback:
loaddata_time = 0.001 * ((num_ports / 8) ** 2)
```

This formula has no validated basis. On a slow VM or loaded CI box it will severely underestimate, giving a budget that is far too small and causing spurious failures. If the baseline measurement fails, either `pytest.skip("Could not measure loadData baseline on this platform")` or use a conservative constant (e.g., 1.0s). Don't silently substitute a quadratic guess.

###  Misleading wording in assertion failure message

```python
"Actual loads/move ratio: {:.1f}x (expected ~2x)."
```

Your own docstring says the safety multiplier accounts for `DeleteRefsMoveExtender` also calling `loadData`. "Expected ~2x" directly contradicts that. Remove the "expected ~2x" and just report the measured ratio.

### Use `expect_op_success()` instead of raw `rc` check

Existing pattern across all GCU tests:
```python
expect_op_success(duthost, output)   # checks rc AND "Patch applied successfully" in stdout
```

This PR uses:
```python
pytest_assert(not output['rc'], "Failed to add test ACL table: ...")
```

`expect_op_success()` catches cases where `rc=0` but the patch silently failed (no success message). Use the standard helper — but verify the current `expect_op_success` signature in `gu_utils.py` before changing.

### Minor — Unique ACL table name

`test_table_name = "GCU_PERF_TEST"` is hardcoded. If a device under test happens to already have a table with this name, the setup patch will conflict. Consider appending a timestamp or short random suffix and using that name consistently through the full test lifecycle.

**In summary** — the 3 bugs will cause hard failures in real runs. The next items are correctness/stability risks. Please validate every change against the present `gu_utils.py` contract and existing test patterns in `tests/generic_config_updater/` before modifying anything. Only change what is actually broken on the target platforms you intend this to run on.

@rimunagala Thanks for the detailed review. Addressed the valid points and pushed back where appropriate:

### Fixed ✅

1. **`elapsed` undefined on exception** — Wrapped `_apply_and_measure` calls in try/except, initialized `elapsed = None`, and use `pytest.fail()` on exception so the real error surfaces clearly.

2. **Missing `format_json_patch_for_multiasic()`** — Added. All patches now go through `format_json_patch_for_multiasic(duthost, patch, is_host_specific=True)` before `apply_patch()`.

3. **Use `expect_op_success()`** — Replaced raw `rc` checks with `expect_op_success(duthost, output)` which also validates "Patch applied successfully" in stdout.

4. **Unique ACL table names** — Table prefix now includes a UUID suffix per test run: `GCU_PERF_<uuid6>`.

5. **Removed "expected ~2x"** — Assertion message now just reports measured values without misleading expectations.

6. **Fallback loadData estimate** — Changed from quadratic guess to a conservative constant (`1.0s`). This makes the budget generous (avoids false failures) while still catching catastrophic regressions. We disagree with `pytest.skip` — skipping means the test provides zero signal. A conservative overestimate still catches 10x+ regressions, which is the whole point of this test.

### Kept as-is (with rationale)

7. **`admin_status='up'` filter** — On properly configured testbeds (which is the target for sonic-mgmt tests), ports are admin up after minigraph loads. The filter ensures we only bind ACL tables to ports that are actually configured. The test has `pytest_assert(len(up_ports) >= 8)` as a guard — if a testbed truly has no admin-up ports, it will fail with a clear message rather than silently testing with phantom ports.

8. **Post-#4478 behavior** — After `BulkLeafListMoveGenerator` lands, the leaf-list REPLACE will be handled as a single move instead of N REMOVEs. The test budget is computed as `FIXED_OVERHEAD + N * 2 * loaddata_time * 5x`, which is generous enough that both code paths (batched and unbatched) must finish within it. The test catches regressions in either path — if the batched path somehow takes longer than N individual moves would under the budget, something is wrong. The multi-op test (test 4) provides additional coverage with mixed operations.

Re-reviewed against `7705219`. Thanks for the thorough turnaround @deepak-singhal0408 — every one of my prior items is addressed, and a couple are addressed more thoroughly than I asked. Walking through them:

### ✅ 1. Topology marker — I was wrong, withdrawing
Verified `tests/common/plugins/custom_markers/__init__.py:69-74`:
```python
topo_marks = [mark for mark in item.iter_markers(name="topology")]
if topo_marks:
    topo_mark = topo_marks[0]   # The nearest mark overrides others
```
`iter_markers` returns nearest-first (function → class → module), so the function-level `@pytest.mark.topology('t0','t1','t2')` correctly overrides the module-level `('t0','t1')`. Your `--collectonly` evidence confirms it. Apologies for the noise on this one.

### ✅ 2. Skip list / normalization
`_verify_config_file_forward` now keeps only the genuinely-FRR-defaulted patterns (`password`, `interface`, `link-detect`, `zebra nexthop kernel`):
- `^network\s+` removed — replaced by `_normalize_network_line` (lines 274-288) that canonicalises via `ipaddress.ip_network(strict=False)`, so `network fc00:1::32/64` correctly matches FRR's `network fc00:1::/64`.
- `^maximum-paths\s+` removed — replaced by per-rendered-file dynamic detection (lines 316-323): only the *specific* `maximum-paths N` value FRR suppresses (build-time `MULTIPATH_NUM`) is skipped; non-default values are still verified.

This is stronger than what I asked for — nice.

### ✅ 3. Backup tracking
Lines 509-518 + 581-583: `backed_up = []`, `pytest.fail` early on backup failure, restore only successfully-backed-up containers. The pollution-on-backup-failure path is closed.

### ✅ 4. Unified-mode render-chain check
Lines 547-554: on iteration 1, after the `wait_until(STRESS_MARKER in running-config)` gate, the test reads `/etc/frr/frr.conf` (or `bgpd.conf`) and confirms the marker is in the rendered file; otherwise `pytest.skip` with a clear message. A unified-mode run where the include chain doesn't reach the template will now skip cleanly rather than silently report success.

### ✅ 5. Tunable iterations
Lines 192-197 + 479-483: `STRESS_ITERATION_LEVEL_MAP = {'debug':1, 'basic':2, 'confident':5, 'thorough':10}`, wired through `get_function_completeness_level`. Nightly defaults to `basic` (~6 min), `thorough` available for weekend/manual runs. Matches the existing `ITERATION_LEVEL_MAP` pattern in the same module.

### Re: @vidyac86's nit ("forward check OK before checking missing")
Looking at the current flow at lines 563-571:
```python
missing = _verify_config_file_forward(...)
if missing:
    for line in missing[:20]:
        logger.error("  MISSING: %s", line)
    pytest.fail("Iter {}, ASIC {}: {} config lines missing ...".format(...))
logger.info("Iter %d, ASIC %s: forward check OK", iteration, asic_id)
```
The "forward check OK" log is unreachable when `missing` is non-empty (`pytest.fail` raises). So the message is emitted only on actual success — the nit no longer applies.

### Verdict
✅ **Approving.** Test design is sound (the forward check across *all* meaningful config lines, including baseline `bgp router-id`, is exactly what catches the production silent-partial-load failure mode). The only purely-optional follow-ups I noticed — none blocking:

1. `bgp_name = 'bgp{}'.format(asic_id)` (line 534) assumes contiguous `asic_id`-named BGP containers; a future chassis with non-contiguous asic_ids could break this. Looking up via `asic.get_docker_name('bgp')` would be safer but is not required for the supported platforms here.
2. `r'^zebra nexthop kernel'` appears both in the module-level `SKIP_CONFIG_COMMANDS` (line 21, used by the legacy test) and in the per-function `skip_patterns` (line 308, used by the new stress test). Harmless duplication, could be shared later.

Thanks again for sticking with the iteration on this — the test is in good shape.

Thanks for the thorough review @StormLiangMS..  comments inline..

> Thanks for adding this — the silent partial-FRR-config-load failure mode is a real production pain and a regression guard is welcome. CI is green and the manual T2 chassis run is good evidence. A few things worth addressing before merge:
> 
> ### 1. Topology marker likely suppresses t2
> The module declares `pytestmark = [pytest.mark.topology('t0', 't1'), …]`, and you're adding `@pytest.mark.topology('t0', 't1', 't2')` at the function level. In sonic-mgmt's custom marker resolver the function-level mark does **not** reliably override the module-level one — the intersection is usually taken, so the new test will most likely _only_ run on t0/t1 in nightly. Your physical-T2 result was a manual invocation, which bypasses this filter.
> 
> Either split the new test into its own file with its own `pytestmark`, or restructure the module-level mark. Worth confirming via a dry `pytest --collect-only` against a t2 inventory.
[DS]: Verified this— function-level marker correctly overrides module-level. tests/common/plugins/custom_markers/__init__.py L70-74 uses topo_marks[0] (nearest marker from iter_markers which returns function → class → module order). No intersection logic. 
Confirmed via pytest --collect-only --topology t2 — test IS collected. 
> 
> ### 2. `_verify_config_file_forward` skip list hides exactly the failures we care about
> ```python
> skip_patterns = [
>     r'^password\s+',
>     r'^interface\s+',
>     r'^link-detect$',
>     r'^network\s+',          # <-- problem
>     r'^maximum-paths\s+',    # <-- problem
>     r'^zebra nexthop kernel',
> ]
> ```
> 
> `^network\s+` and `^maximum-paths\s+` are precisely the kind of lines a _partial config load_ would silently drop — which is the bug class this whole test exists to catch. Skipping them undercuts the test's stated value. `^interface\s+` also drops a whole config block including any inner directives.
> 
> Suggest:
> 
> * Keep skip list narrow (only `password` and lines FRR genuinely never echoes).
> * For `network` / `maximum-paths`, use a forgiving normalization (token-wise / prefix-match against running-config) rather than skipping outright.
> * Add a one-line comment per skip explaining _why FRR drops this line in `show running-config`_, with a reference, so future maintainers don't widen the list to make a flake go away.
> 
[DS]: Agreed and addressed. Removed ^network\s+ and ^maximum-paths\s+ from static skip. Now:
  - network lines → prefix-normalized via ipaddress.ip_network(strict=False) before comparison (handles FRR's %pFX 
canonicalization, e.g. fc00:1::32/64 → fc00:1::/64)
  - maximum-paths → dynamic detection: scans rendered config for maximum-paths N, checks if N is absent from 
running-config (meaning FRR suppressed it because N == compile-time MULTIPATH_NUM), and only skips that specific value.  Non-default values are verified normally.
  - Added per-pattern comments explaining why each remaining skip exists.
 
> ### 3. Cleanup: backup-failure path can leave a polluted template
> The `finally` block restores both backups unconditionally with `module_ignore_errors=True`. That's safe when both `docker cp` backups succeeded, but if the _backup_ itself fails for container N (disk full, container restart, etc.) and injection still happens to land later, the restore quietly copies a non-existent file and the polluted `bgpd.main.conf.j2` survives into the next test on this DUT.
> 
> Suggest tracking which `(container, backup_path)` pairs were successfully created, restore only those, and `pytest.fail` early if a backup itself fails so we don't proceed to inject.
> 
[DS]: Fixed. Now tracks successful backups in a backed_up list, pytest.fails early if any backup fails, and
 finally only restores containers in backed_up.

> ### 4. Unified mode: `bgpd.main.conf.j2` may not be on the render path
> In unified mode the BGP container renders `frr.conf` from `frr.conf.j2`. On some branches/devices `frr.conf.j2` doesn't `{% include %}` `bgpd.main.conf.j2` — it inlines or pulls from different sub-templates. Modifying `bgpd.main.conf.j2` in unified mode could then be a no-op, the forward check would still trivially pass against an unstressed config, and we'd think we tested unified when we didn't.
> 
> Worth a precondition check — e.g. `grep -l bgpd.main.conf.j2 /usr/share/sonic/templates/bgpd/*.j2 /usr/share/sonic/templates/frr.conf.j2` inside the container, and `pytest.skip` with a clear message if the include chain doesn't reach it. Alternatively, after injection, assert the stress marker shows up in the rendered file (`/etc/frr/frr.conf` or `/etc/frr/bgpd.conf`) before counting that iteration as a valid test.
> 
[DS]: Investigated — bgpd.main.conf.j2 IS in the render chain for unified mode on master (frr.conf.j2 line 25:
 {% include "bgpd/bgpd.main.conf.j2" %}). 
Added a defensive check anyway: on first iteration, verifies STRESS_MARKER is present in the rendered file (/etc/frr/frr.conf). If not, pytest.skips with a clear message rather than silently passing.

> ### 5. Make iteration count tunable via `get_function_completeness_level`
> 5 × `config_reload(safe_reload=True, check_intf_up_ports=True)` per ASIC plus a final cleanup reload is ~15–25 min on KVM and substantially longer on hardware. The existing `test_frr_config_check` already scales iterations (`'thorough': 50`). Suggest the same pattern here:
> 
> ```python
> NUM_STRESS_ITERATIONS = {
>     'debug': 1,
>     'basic': 2,
>     'confident': 3,
>     'thorough': 5,
> }
> ```

[DS]: Done. Added STRESS_ITERATION_LEVEL_MAP following existing pattern. basic (nightly) = 2 iterations 
(6 min), thorough = 10 (30 min).

> 
> Lets nightly run a shorter version and weekend / manual runs do the full stress without forking the test.
> 
> None of these are blocking the _idea_ — test motivation and overall structure look good. Happy to re-review once 1, 4 and the skip-list change in 2 are addressed.

@xwjiang-ms — review comments below. Solid v1 scope and the right overall shape; a few things to tighten before this lands.

## Blocking issues

**1. Section 5 file paths are inconsistent with reality**
Section 5 shows the doc as `docs/test-cherry-pick-policy.md`, but the file actually added is `docs/tests/design-doc-test-cherry-pick-guard.md` — different name and different path. Pick one and align.

**2. Missing required-check name specification**
Section 6 → Branch Protection requires a status check named **`Cherry-pick policy check`**, but nowhere does the doc define what `name:` the workflow / job will use. That string is what GitHub branch protection matches verbatim. If implementation diverges by even one character, enforcement silently breaks. Please pin the canonical workflow `name:` and `job.<id>.name:` in Section 5 so they're frozen by review.

**3. Open Question #1 is a blocker, not an open question**
"Which GitHub team slug is the authoritative platform-owners for sonic-mgmt?" is the keystone of the entire authorization model. Without it, the workflow can't be implemented. Resolve, or at minimum commit to a concrete placeholder team that gets re-pointed later, before merging.

**4. SMTP outage = release branches blocked for all new-test cherry-picks**
If `GUARD_NOTIFICATION_EMAIL` SMTP send fails, the `enforce-policy` job presumably exits non-zero → required check fails → no bypass possible until SMTP recovers. This isn't addressed. Either:
- make the email send best-effort (log on failure, don't fail the check), and document that a missed email is an audit gap, not a merge gate, or
- explicitly document that SMTP outage disables the bypass path and add a runbook entry.

## Important (non-blocking)

**5. "Checks API" remnants after label-only simplification**
Commit `0a0005863` removed the Checks API workaround when bypass was simplified to label-only. But Section 3.1 diagram still says *"PASS → create/update check run on PR HEAD via Checks API"* and Section 6 permissions table doesn't list `checks: write`. Either remove the Checks API mention from the diagram or add the permission — currently internally inconsistent.

**6. State machine posts violation comment on every initial PR open**
Section 3.4: a brand-new cherry-pick PR with new tests fails the policy and posts a "policy violation comment" before any reviewer has had a chance to look at it. This will be noisy on every cherry-pick PR with new tests. Either suppress on `opened` and only post on `synchronize` after a grace, or make it idempotent (only post once, never re-post on subsequent runs — same mechanism as the email marker). The doc says "(once, idempotent)" but doesn't describe a marker for the violation comment, only for the success email. Spell out the marker for both.

**7. `contains-new-tests` label is never removed on force-push**
If a contributor force-pushes to remove the offending new test files, the `contains-new-tests` label stays and Job 2 keeps requiring a bypass. Specify: on each `synchronize`, if `has_new_tests == false`, remove the label and clear the bypass-required state.

**8. Behavior on `unlabeled` of `cherry-pick-bypass-approved` should be explicit**
Implicit but should be stated: if someone removes the bypass label after a successful pass, the next workflow run reverts to FAIL, restoring the merge block. Worth spelling out — it's a useful kill switch and reviewers should understand it.

**9. Open Question #4 (PAT vs GitHub App) blocks Section 6**
Section 6 currently documents both options as valid. Until #4 is resolved, you can't write the actual workflow. Decide and record. Recommendation: GitHub App — PATs tied to individuals are an audit nightmare and break when the user leaves; App tokens scope cleanly to the repo.

## Minor / editorial

**10. Test file pattern is too restrictive**
Section 3.3 only matches `tests/**/test_*.py`. Pytest also discovers `*_test.py` per default config; check `tests/pytest.ini` / `tests/conftest.py` for the actual discovery patterns. If the repo uses non-default discovery, the guard will miss real test files.

**11. Rename detection gap is acknowledged but not classified**
Section 3.3's "Known limitation: renames" leaves it ambiguous whether a copy-then-rename evades the guard. Add an explicit example: "Cherry-picks created via `git format-patch` typically surface as `added`; cherry-picks of a prior rename can surface as `renamed`. v1 misses the latter."

**12. Timeline API eventual consistency**
Section 3.5 uses the timeline API to find the most recent `labeled` event. That endpoint has multi-second eventual-consistency lag in practice. Either use `github.event.sender.login` (the user who triggered the labeled event) or add a retry loop on the timeline lookup.

**13. Phase 3 verification ordering bug**
Section 8: Phase 3 ("verify bypass flow") happens before Phase 4 ("add branch protection"). Without protection, the test PR will merge regardless of what the check says, so Phase 3 doesn't actually verify the *block*. Swap Phase 3 and Phase 4, or add an explicit "confirm check status is failing" step inside Phase 3 before the bypass label is added.

**14. Authorization API doesn't handle child teams**
Section 3.5 calls `GET /orgs/{org}/teams/{team}/memberships/{login}` which by default checks **direct** membership only. If `platform-owners` ever adds child teams, members of child teams won't authorize. Either restrict to flat team membership (and document it) or use the broader permission check.

**15. "Bot" unspecified in audit comment**
Section 3.7 says "Audit comment by bot". With a PAT, comments come from the PAT owner's account; with a GitHub App, from `<app-name>[bot]`. This affects how reviewers visually parse the comment and how filters / notification rules match. Specify which.

**16. No metrics / dashboards proposed**
There's no plan for tracking bypass frequency over time. Without it, the team has no signal that the policy is being abused or that v2's explanation comment requirement is needed. Add a Section 11 "Observability": e.g., monthly Kusto query against the audit comments, or label counts.

**17. `merge_group` event not addressed**
If sonic-mgmt enables GitHub Merge Queues, the workflow needs `merge_group` trigger or merges will bypass the check. Worth a one-liner in Section 3.2.

**18. Missing newline at EOF** (cosmetic — `\ No newline at end of file` in the diff).

**19. `[AWARENESS]` subject prefix** — consider `[ACTION]` or `[FYI]` to match common Outlook filter patterns; "AWARENESS" is unusual and harder to filter on. Personal taste, not blocking.

## Bottom line

Three things to address before merge: fix the file-path inconsistency in Section 5 (#1), pin down the required-check name (#2), and either resolve or concretely placeholder the `platform-owners` team identity (#3). The SMTP-outage failure mode (#4) needs at least a documented stance. The rest are nits / future-proofing.
